text: Everlastingly. Is such a short-lived creature an object worthy of thy eternal wrath? (Job 13:25., and 14:1., and Psalm 102:13.)

Dead; banished, (ver. 11., and Ezechiel 37:5.) or of the ancient patriarchs. This motive is frequently urged, Exodus 32:13., Leviticus 26:41., Deuteronomy 9:27., Daniel 3:35., and Jeremias 15:1. (Calmet)<hr>People in sin and misery seem dead, (ver. 11.) yet may revive by grace. (Worthington)
Hand. Save us by thy power, and for thy own name's sake, Josue 7:9.
Offence. Greek, "debt." Parents might sell their children, Matthew 18:25. God threatens his rebellious people, that they shall be forced to borrow, Deuteronomy 28:44. (Calmet)

Hear. God replies. (Haydock)<hr>The Church has long read this has holy scripture, on the eves of Easter and Pentecost. (Worthington)<hr>This second part contains an instruction respecting true wisdom; which is to be found in God alone, (Calmet) and in the people to whom he is pleased to communicate it. (Haydock)

Grown. Houbigant, "growing." Five years had not elapsed: (Cappellus) yet they knew their captivity would be long.<hr>Dead. Some might yield to idolatry, Daniel 3:7, 12. (Haydock)<hr>They were in a manner buried, (Psalm 87:5.) and forced to dwell among people where they were exposed to many defilements, as if they had been near a dead body, Leviticus 11:25.
Peace. There is none for the wicked; but only for the observers of the law.<hr>For ever. Many read, "upon the land," conformably to the Greek.
Treasures? How few are truly wise and virtuous! The great men of the world have missed their aim. Israel has received the law. Yet where is the fruit of it? This is developed in the sequel.
Nations of Assyria? etc. They esteemed themselves as lords of nature, and delighted in hunting. The beasts and birds are subject to them, Genesis 1:26., Jeremias 27:6., and Daniel 2:38. (Calmet)
Unsearchable? Shall they not find the fruit of their works? (Worthington)
Men; heirs of those conquerors, and equally in the dark. (Calmet)
Theman, the capital city of Edom. (Challoner)<hr>Eliphaz was king, Jeremias 49:7.<hr>The Phoenicians, so famous for industry, had no idea of true wisdom, no more than the Ismaelites, etc. (Calmet)

Agar, the mother of the Ismaelites. (Challoner)<hr>Merrha, probably in Arabia.<hr>Fables. This may be a reproach, or a commendation. Those nations delighted in enigmatical and sententious discourses, 3 Kings 10:1. (Calmet)<hr>Instructors of mankind deserve praise, not those who devise false gods for lucre. (St. Augustine, City of God 6:5., etc.) (Worthington)
Possession. All the world belongs to him: yet how few partake of his wisdom!
Giants, before and after the deluge, Genesis vi. Noe[Noah] and Israel were preferred before them.
Clouds? No human industry can procure this blessing. God alone is the dispenser of it, Deuteronomy 30:12. (Calmet)<hr>His grace makes the discovery easy, Matthew 11:30. (Worthington)
Evermore. Literally, "in the eternal time," or so long ago; (Haydock) designed to continue, while kingdoms shall change, Ecclesiastes 1:4.
Trembling. The sun stops, goes back, or withdraws its light, at his command, Josue 10:12., and 4 Kings 20:9., Matthew 27:45., and Job 36:30.
Watches. They are like his soldiers, Judges 5:20., and Ecclesiasticus 43:12. (Calmet)

Earth, etc., by the mystery of the incarnation, by means of which the son of God came visibly amongst us, and conversed with men. The prophets often speak of things to come as if they were past, to express the certainty of the event of the things foretold. (Challoner) (Calmet)<hr>Most of the Fathers prove Christ's divinity from this text. (Worthington) See Tertullian, contra Prax. 6.; St. Cyprian, contra Jud. 2:5.; Eusebius, Dem. 6:19.<hr>If we explain it of wisdom, it comes to the same purpose, as Christ is the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:30. Compare Proverbs 8:30., Ecclesiasticus 24:12., and Wisdom 9:10., which no one ever suspected to be interpolated. (Calmet) (Preface)
